entries.body:
---
Week 10 Reflection: The HRTO Got Its Reasoning Wrong — and the Court Said So
Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 17, 2026
This week in class: Standard of review — choosing and applying. The Vavilov framework. What reasonableness actually demands from a decision-maker. When do courts intervene, and when do they defer?
---
On March 17 — the first class day of our standard of review week — the Ontario Divisional Court released Burland v Precise ParkLink Inc, 2026 ONSC 1587.
The Court quashed an HRTO decision that had dismissed a human rights application. The reason: the Tribunal’s decision was unreasonable under the Vavilov standard. Its reasons were “peremptory” — brief, conclusory, and devoid of the justification, transparency, and intelligibility that Vavilov demands.
The matter was sent back to the Tribunal to be decided again.
---
What Vavilov actually asks of decision-makers
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 restructured how Canadian courts review administrative decisions. The default is now a presumption of reasonableness — courts start by deferring to the decision-maker’s expertise and mandate.
But the presumption has teeth. Vavilov defined reasonableness not as “any defensible outcome” but as a decision that is:
Justified: the decision-maker must explain why they reached the outcome, engaging with the key arguments and evidence
Transparent: the reasoning must be visible and traceable, not hidden in a black box
Intelligible: the logic must be coherent — the conclusion must follow from the reasoning
And the reasoning must be responsive to the actual arguments made by the parties. A decision that simply states a conclusion without engaging with the losing party’s case is not just thin — it may be unreasonable.
---
Why this matters for human rights adjudication
The HRTO handles some of the most important individual rights claims in Ontario. Dismissing an application — telling a person that their human rights complaint does not merit a hearing — is a significant exercise of public power.
When that dismissal comes with “peremptory reasons” that give the applicant no understanding of why their complaint failed, it does something more than fail the Vavilov standard. It fails the person.
Procedural fairness (which we studied in Week 8) includes the right to reasons. Vavilov makes the quality of those reasons a matter of substantive reasonableness. These two doctrines reinforce each other: a decision-maker who does not explain itself is both procedurally unfair and substantively unreasonable.
---
The bigger picture: deference with accountability
The standard of review framework is sometimes misread as courts staying out of administrative decisions. It is not. Vavilov is a framework for structured deference — deference calibrated to the quality of the decision-maker’s reasoning.
The more expert the decision-maker, the more their judgments on specialized questions should be respected. But the more they must show their work. Expertise without explanation is not enough. Vavilov made that explicit.
Watching the Divisional Court apply Vavilov to the HRTO in real time, this week of all weeks, felt like the course coming full circle: the law we have been studying all semester, working exactly as it is supposed to.
---
See also: Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 · Human Rights Tribunal of Ontario · HRTO 2025 Case Law in Review — Ontario Bar Association

author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Is the OPGT an Administrative Decision-Maker?

The Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee (OPGT) is a provincial statutory official who makes legally binding decisions about the property and personal care of vulnerable Ontarians — people who are mentally incapable and have no one to act for them. Yes, the OPGT is an administrative decision-maker: it is created by statute, exercises compulsory public authority over some of the most consequential decisions in a person's life, and its decisions are subject to judicial oversight.

entries.body:
---
Is the OPGT an Administrative Decision-Maker?
TL;DR
The Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee (OPGT) is a provincial statutory official who makes legally binding decisions about the property and personal care of vulnerable Ontarians — people who are mentally incapable and have no one to act for them. Yes, the OPGT is an administrative decision-maker: it is created by statute, exercises compulsory public authority over some of the most consequential decisions in a person's life, and its decisions are subject to judicial oversight.
---
Why this question matters
Most administrative law discussions focus on familiar regulatory bodies — licensing agencies, immigration boards, employment tribunals. But some of the most consequential administrative decisions happen in a far less visible place: the management of a person's financial affairs and personal care when they can no longer make decisions for themselves.
Ontario's OPGT is the government's answer to a hard governance problem: who decides for the person who cannot decide for themselves, when there is no family member or trusted person to step in?
The answer turns out to be a statutory office of the Crown — one that exercises powers over property, finances, and medical treatment affecting thousands of Ontarians every year. It is worth understanding what kind of legal actor it is, and what that means for the people subject to its authority.
---
1. What is the OPGT?
The Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee is a corporation sole — a legal entity that exists independently of the specific individual holding the office, vested in the Attorney General of Ontario.
Its primary enabling statute is the Substitute Decisions Act, 1992, SO 1992, c 30, which governs who may make decisions about property and personal care on behalf of mentally incapable adults in Ontario.
The OPGT also draws authority from:
Children's Law Reform Act — acting as litigation guardian for children in court proceedings
Estates Act — administering estates where no executor or administrator is available
Mental Health Act — financial management of patients in psychiatric facilities
Charities Accounting Act — oversight of charitable property
---
2. What decisions does the OPGT make?
The OPGT makes binding decisions in three main domains.
A. Statutory property guardianship (SDA, ss.15–22)
When an adult is found mentally incapable of managing property — and has no valid power of attorney and no court-appointed guardian — the OPGT automatically becomes their statutory guardian of property by operation of statute. No court order is needed. No application is required.
As statutory guardian, OPGT:
Manages bank accounts, investments, pensions, and real estate
Pays bills, collects income, and files tax returns
Makes financial decisions in the person's "best interests"
Must prepare and implement a formal management plan (SDA, s.32)
B. Personal care decisions (SDA, s.27 and Health Care Consent Act, 1996)
In urgent situations where a mentally incapable person's personal care is at risk and no substitute decision-maker is available, the OPGT may authorize personal care decisions — including medical treatment.
This is the most intimate form of administrative power: a government official deciding what medical treatment another person receives. The Health Care Consent Act, 1996 provides the framework, with the Consent and Capacity Board as the appellate review body.
C. Litigation guardianship
The OPGT acts as litigation guardian for parties under a legal disability — including minors and mentally incapable adults — who are parties to court proceedings and have no suitable private representative available.
---
3. Is the OPGT an administrative decision-maker?
Yes, unambiguously. Applying the standard definition:
✅ Delegated authority? Yes — the Substitute Decisions Act, 1992 expressly creates the OPGT and vests statutory guardianship powers in the office. Authority flows directly from the legislature, not from contract or private arrangement.
✅ Affects rights and interests? Profoundly. OPGT decisions control another person's bank accounts, investments, real property, and medical treatment. Few administrative decisions have more direct and intimate impact on an individual's daily life.
✅ Public in character? Yes — the OPGT is a Crown corporation sole, performing a mandatory statutory function assigned by the province. It does not contract with its "clients"; it is assigned jurisdiction by operation of law.
The OPGT is not a licensing or regulatory body in the traditional sense — but the definition of ADM is not limited to those forms. Any body exercising delegated statutory authority that affects the rights and interests of individuals qualifies.
---
4. Applying the Wall test
In Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26, the SCC confirmed that judicial review is available only when (1) there is an exercise of state authority and (2) that exercise is of sufficiently public character.
State authority? The OPGT's powers arise entirely from statute — there is no contract, no voluntary submission, no private arrangement. The OPGT becomes a person's property guardian by operation of the Substitute Decisions Act alone. This is state authority in its most direct form.
Sufficiently public character? Using the Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority contextual factors:
OPGT's decisions are entirely shaped by statute, not private discretion
It exercises compulsory powers — the affected person has not chosen the OPGT and cannot dismiss it
It is accountable to the Attorney General and through the AG to the legislature
Its mandate — protecting vulnerable citizens who cannot protect themselves — is paradigmatically public in dimension
Both Wall criteria are clearly satisfied. OPGT decisions are subject to judicial review.
---
5. Accountability and oversight
Unlike a private trustee — accountable through common law fiduciary duties to a court — the OPGT's accountability operates through multiple layers:

---
6. The deeper tension: power without agency
What makes the OPGT uniquely significant in administrative law is not just that it is an ADM — it is that its clients are, by definition, people who cannot advocate for themselves.
The procedural fairness protections that Baker and Vavilov build presuppose a party who can receive notice, participate in a process, and respond to proposed decisions. When the person whose rights are affected lacks legal capacity to participate, the duty of fairness takes on a different character:
Who represents the person's genuine interests, as distinct from their formal legal interests?
How does an ADM prevent drift from "best interests" to institutional convenience or resource constraints?
Does the duty of fairness require the OPGT to seek out and give weight to the incapable person's expressed wishes — even wishes that predate incapacity?
These are not hypothetical concerns. They are live legal and ethical tensions in substitute decision-making law, and they point to a fundamental challenge in administrative law: how to protect procedural fairness when the affected party cannot enforce it themselves.
---
Key takeaways
The OPGT is an ADM: a Crown corporation sole exercising delegated statutory authority over some of the most intimate decisions in a person's life.
Its jurisdiction is automatic — the OPGT becomes property guardian by operation of statute, without a court order or the incapable person's consent.
Both Wall criteria are met: OPGT decisions involve direct state authority and are paradigmatically public in character.
Accountability is multi-layered: management plans, the Consent and Capacity Board, Superior Court review, and judicial review all apply.
The deepest tension is substantive: ensuring that "best interest" substituted decisions genuinely serve the incapable person — not institutional efficiency or administrative convenience.
---
Sources and further reading
Legislation
Substitute Decisions Act, 1992, SO 1992, c 30
Health Care Consent Act, 1996
Mental Health Act (Ontario)
Children's Law Reform Act (Ontario)
Estates Act (Ontario)
Key cases
Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26 — threshold test for judicial review
Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40 — procedural fairness and ADM obligations
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 — standard of review
Further reading
Office of the Public Guardian and Trustee — Ontario
Consent and Capacity Board — Tribunals Ontario
Substitute Decisions Act Guide — Government of Ontario

author: Fukun

rich_text.text: A Weekly Admin Law Observation · Osgoode Hall · Winter 2026

AGCO is unambiguously an administrative decision-maker. It is created by statute, receives its powers from the Ontario legislature, exercises compulsory public authority over licensing and penalties, and its decisions are subject to judicial review. Three intuitive-sounding arguments against this conclusion all fail on first principles of administrative law.
If you have ever bought alcohol, visited a casino, purchased cannabis from a licensed retailer, or bet on a horse race in Ontario, your transaction was regulated by
the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario (AGCO)
The AGCO touches everyday commercial life across the province. Whether it qualifies as an

(ADM) is not just a classification exercise, it determines whether its decisions are subject to the full force of administrative law: the duty of procedural fairness, substantive review for reasonableness, and the possibility of judicial review at the Superior Court.

an agent of the Crown in right of Ontario,
firmly placing it within the executive branch of government.

Provincial Cabinet appoints board members and sets their remuneration

Delegated authority? Yes, ss. 2 and 3 of the
expressly grant regulatory and administrative powers.
Affects rights and interests? Yes,AGCO issues, refuses, suspends, and revokes licences; imposes monetary penalties; and enforces compliance obligations with real economic consequences.
Public in character? Yes, regulates publicly important industries (alcohol, cannabis, gaming) under compulsory provincial authority.
AGCO is an ADM. So is its Registrar.
Both exercise delegated statutory powers that affect legal rights.
3. Three misconceptions — and why they fail
When examining whether AGCO is an ADM, three intuitive arguments arise. All three are wrong.
❌ Misconception A: "Cabinet appoints the board, so AGCO can't be an ADM"
"Only the legislature can appoint administrative decision-makers and set their salaries."
This reverses how the administrative state actually works. ADMs are part of the

— and Cabinet is the executive. It is entirely normal, expected, and lawful for Cabinet to appoint board members and set their compensation. That is how virtually every tribunal, regulator, and Crown agency in Canada operates.
The legislature creates the body and delegates the power. Cabinet administers and staffs it. Neither function disqualifies the other. AGCO's
obligations — to stay within its statutory mandate and exercise its powers in good faith — exist independently of who appoints its members.
❌ Misconception B: "LAT hears appeals from the Registrar, so the Registrar can't be an ADM too"
"Because the Licence Appeal Tribunal is an administrative decision-maker, the Registrar cannot also be an administrative actor."
This misunderstands how tiered administrative systems work. Canadian administrative law is full of layered structures where
one ADM hears appeals from another ADM
. Think of the Immigration and Refugee Board and the Refugee Appeal Division, or the WSIB and the Workplace Safety and Insurance Appeals Tribunal.

The existence of a downstream appeal body does not retroactively strip the first-instance decision-maker of its character. Both the AGCO Registrar (first-instance) and LAT (appellate) are ADMs operating at different levels of the same statutory scheme.
❌ Misconception C: "AGCO hasn't been delegated power by the legislature"

expressly create the Commission and delegate regulatory authority over alcohol, cannabis, gaming, and horse racing to it. The Commission openly describes itself as a provincial regulatory agency. Calling this "not a delegation" would contradict the plain text of the statute.

---
Is AGCO an Administrative Decision-Maker?
A Weekly Admin Law Observation · Osgoode Hall · Winter 2026
TL;DR
AGCO is unambiguously an administrative decision-maker. It is created by statute, receives its powers from the Ontario legislature, exercises compulsory public authority over licensing and penalties, and its decisions are subject to judicial review. Three intuitive-sounding arguments against this conclusion all fail on first principles of administrative law.
---
Why this question matters
If you have ever bought alcohol, visited a casino, purchased cannabis from a licensed retailer, or bet on a horse race in Ontario, your transaction was regulated by the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario (AGCO).
The AGCO touches everyday commercial life across the province. Whether it qualifies as an administrative decision-maker (ADM) is not just a classification exercise, it determines whether its decisions are subject to the full force of administrative law: the duty of procedural fairness, substantive review for reasonableness, and the possibility of judicial review at the Superior Court.
The short answer is: yes, unambiguously. But arriving at that answer requires clearing away three persistent misconceptions.
---
1. What is AGCO? The statutory structure
AGCO is established by s. 2 of the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019, SO 2019, c 15, Sched 1.
Under s. 1.1 (added by 2020 amendment), the Commission is explicitly declared an agent of the Crown in right of Ontario, firmly placing it within the executive branch of government.
Its key structural features under the Act:

In addition to the 2019 Act, AGCO administers a suite of enabling statutes, including the Liquor Licence and Control Act, 2019, the Gaming Control Act, 1992, the Cannabis Licence Act, 2018, and the Horse Racing Licence Act, 2015.
---
2. The administrative decision-maker test
Administrative law defines an ADM as any individual or body that has been delegated decision-making authority by the legislature through an enabling statute, and that exercises powers affecting the rights, interests, or legitimate expectations of others.
The defining feature is the source of power: delegation by the legislature, not the form of the body, its name, or how its members are appointed.
Applying this to AGCO:
Delegated authority? Yes, ss. 2 and 3 of the AGCO Act, 2019 expressly grant regulatory and administrative powers.
Affects rights and interests? Yes,AGCO issues, refuses, suspends, and revokes licences; imposes monetary penalties; and enforces compliance obligations with real economic consequences.
Public in character? Yes, regulates publicly important industries (alcohol, cannabis, gaming) under compulsory provincial authority.
AGCO is an ADM. So is its Registrar. Both exercise delegated statutory powers that affect legal rights.
---
3. Three misconceptions — and why they fail
When examining whether AGCO is an ADM, three intuitive arguments arise. All three are wrong.
❌ Misconception A: "Cabinet appoints the board, so AGCO can't be an ADM"
"Only the legislature can appoint administrative decision-makers and set their salaries."
This reverses how the administrative state actually works. ADMs are part of the executive branch — and Cabinet is the executive. It is entirely normal, expected, and lawful for Cabinet to appoint board members and set their compensation. That is how virtually every tribunal, regulator, and Crown agency in Canada operates.
The legislature creates the body and delegates the power. Cabinet administers and staffs it. Neither function disqualifies the other. AGCO's Roncarelli obligations — to stay within its statutory mandate and exercise its powers in good faith — exist independently of who appoints its members.
❌ Misconception B: "LAT hears appeals from the Registrar, so the Registrar can't be an ADM too"
"Because the Licence Appeal Tribunal is an administrative decision-maker, the Registrar cannot also be an administrative actor."
This misunderstands how tiered administrative systems work. Canadian administrative law is full of layered structures where one ADM hears appeals from another ADM. Think of the Immigration and Refugee Board and the Refugee Appeal Division, or the WSIB and the Workplace Safety and Insurance Appeals Tribunal.
The existence of a downstream appeal body does not retroactively strip the first-instance decision-maker of its character. Both the AGCO Registrar (first-instance) and LAT (appellate) are ADMs operating at different levels of the same statutory scheme.
❌ Misconception C: "AGCO hasn't been delegated power by the legislature"
This is simply factually wrong. Sections 2 and 3 of the AGCO Act, 2019 expressly create the Commission and delegate regulatory authority over alcohol, cannabis, gaming, and horse racing to it. The Commission openly describes itself as a provincial regulatory agency. Calling this "not a delegation" would contradict the plain text of the statute.
---
4. Is AGCO subject to judicial review? Applying Wall
Even if AGCO is an ADM, we still need to ask whether its decisions are subject to judicial review (JR). The governing test comes from Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26:
JR is available only when (1) there is an exercise of state authority, and (2) that exercise is of a sufficiently public character.
Applying Wall to AGCO:
Step 1 — State authority? Clearly yes. AGCO's powers flow directly from Ontario statutes. Its licensing decisions, monetary penalties, and enforcement actions are statutory, not contractual or voluntary. It is not a private club or self-governing professional association acting on its own rules — it is the Crown acting through delegation.
Step 2 — Sufficiently public character? Also yes. Using the contextual factors from Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority, 2011 FCA 347 (Stratas J):
AGCO's decisions are shaped entirely by statute, not private discretion.
It exercises compulsory powers (you cannot operate a licensed establishment without AGCO approval).
It is directed by and accountable to the provincial government.
Its decisions carry major public dimensions — public health, consumer protection, organized crime prevention.
Both Wall criteria are met. AGCO decisions are subject to judicial review.
---
5. The decision chain in practice
Understanding AGCO as an ADM has a direct practical consequence, it means affected parties have a structured legal pathway to challenge decisions:
AGCO Registrar decision (first instance)
        ↓
Licence Appeal Tribunal — LAT
(appeal within 15 days under s. 14(7) AGCO Act, 2019)
        ↓
Judicial Review — Ontario Superior Court of Justice (Divisional Court)
(on questions of law, procedural fairness, or unreasonableness)
The Licence Appeal Tribunal is an independent adjudicative tribunal under Tribunals Ontario. Its decisions on AGCO matters are in turn reviewable by the courts, with the standard of review governed by the Vavilov framework (Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65).
---
6. Not constitutionally independent — and that's the point
One important feature: AGCO board members' salaries are set by Cabinet, their appointments are not security-protected, and the Commission can be restructured by the legislature. This is not a deficiency — it is precisely what confirms AGCO's status as an ADM rather than a court.
Courts enjoy constitutional independence under ss. 96, 99, and 100 of the Constitution Act, 1867. ADMs do not, and are not meant to. They are executive bodies performing regulatory functions. Their accountability runs to the legislature through statute, and to the courts through judicial review — not through constitutional tenure guarantees.
---
Key takeaways
The source of power is everything. An ADM is defined by legislative delegation, not by its name or organizational form.
Cabinet appointment is normal and expected for executive branch bodies — it doesn't undermine ADM status.
Tiered appeals between ADMs are common in Canadian administrative law. One appeal body does not erase the first-instance ADM.
AGCO passes the Wall test on both limbs: state authority and sufficiently public character.
AGCO decisions are judicially reviewable at the Ontario Divisional Court following LAT proceedings, applying the Vavilov reasonableness standard.
---

Legislation
Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019, SO 2019, c 15, Sched 1
Liquor Licence and Control Act, 2019
Gaming Control Act, 1992
Cannabis Licence Act, 2018
Horse Racing Licence Act, 2015
Licence Appeal Tribunal Act, 1999
Key cases
Highwood Congregation of Jehovah's Witnesses v Wall, 2018 SCC 26 — the threshold test for judicial review availability
Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority, 2011 FCA 347 — 8-factor "sufficiently public character" test
Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 1999 SCC 40 (CanLII) — leading case on procedural fairness and ADM obligations
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65 — standard of review framework
[Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1959/1959canscc1/1959canscc1.html) — ADMs must act within their statutory mandate; arbitrary exercises of power are unlawful

entries.title: Week 9 Reflection: Zoom Court and the Apprehension of Bias
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Week 9 Reflection: Zoom Court and the Apprehension of Bias
Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 10, 2026
The rule against bias. Actual bias vs. reasonable apprehension of bias. The
para 46 test. Institutional bias, individual bias, and the conditions that make a fair hearing possible.

Most Ontario administrative hearings are now conducted over Zoom. The LTB, LAT, HRTO, and Consent and Capacity Board all moved to remote hearings during COVID and have largely stayed there. For many applicants, this has improved access — no travel, no childcare logistics, no time off work.
But I have been thinking this week about a different question:
what does Zoom do to the appearance of impartiality?
This week we studied the test for reasonable apprehension of bias from
Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board

The apprehension of bias must be a reasonable one, held by reasonable and right-minded persons, applying themselves to the question and obtaining thereon the required information… [T]he test is whether an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically — and having thought the matter through — would have a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker would not decide fairly.
Note what the test does not require: proof of
What Zoom makes harder to see

In a physical hearing room, the signals of engagement — or disengagement — are visible to everyone. An adjudicator who is looking out the window, or who is visibly impatient with one party’s submissions, or who leans forward when one side speaks and leans back when the other does, sends observable signals.

On Zoom, those signals are either amplified or hidden. An adjudicator with camera off is invisible. One who is multitasking may not be noticed. Pre-hearing written remarks in case management communications can surface in ways that might not have been recorded before.

And the parties themselves are at home, in their own spaces, separated from each other and from the decision-maker. The shared physical experience of a public hearing — which has always been part of what makes adjudication
, not just technically fair — is absent.
Also this week, the Divisional Court issued a decision reinforcing that courts should defer to the
findings on workplace disputes. The case involved a construction industry work assignment dispute — technical, expert-driven, squarely within the OLRB’s institutional competence.
But it reminded me of the flip side of the bias question: tribunals that are

in their field are given deference precisely because* they know things courts don’t. The moment a party starts alleging bias without real evidence, they risk asking a court to substitute its own judgment for that expert judgment — which is not what judicial review is for.
The reasonable apprehension of bias test is calibrated to distinguish genuine fairness concerns from tactical attempts to relitigate outcomes.

As administrative hearings become increasingly digital and informal, the procedural fairness frameworks we have built around in-person adjudication are going to be tested. The bias test — built on the idea of the
watching the process — assumes there is something to observe.
Zoom hearings are good for access. Whether they are equally good for perceived legitimacy is a question I don’t think the doctrine has fully answered yet.

---
Week 9 Reflection: Zoom Court and the Apprehension of Bias
Admin Law in Daily Life · Week of March 10, 2026
This week in class: The rule against bias. Actual bias vs. reasonable apprehension of bias. The Baker para 46 test. Institutional bias, individual bias, and the conditions that make a fair hearing possible.
---
Most Ontario administrative hearings are now conducted over Zoom. The LTB, LAT, HRTO, and Consent and Capacity Board all moved to remote hearings during COVID and have largely stayed there. For many applicants, this has improved access — no travel, no childcare logistics, no time off work.
But I have been thinking this week about a different question: what does Zoom do to the appearance of impartiality?
---
The Baker para 46 test
This week we studied the test for reasonable apprehension of bias from Baker para 46 (citing Committee for Justice and Liberty v National Energy Board [1978] 1 SCR 369):
The apprehension of bias must be a reasonable one, held by reasonable and right-minded persons, applying themselves to the question and obtaining thereon the required information… [T]he test is whether an informed person, viewing the matter realistically and practically — and having thought the matter through — would have a reasonable apprehension that the decision-maker would not decide fairly.
Note what the test does not require: proof of actual bias. It requires only a reasonable apprehension by a well-informed, realistic observer.
---
What Zoom makes harder to see
In a physical hearing room, the signals of engagement — or disengagement — are visible to everyone. An adjudicator who is looking out the window, or who is visibly impatient with one party’s submissions, or who leans forward when one side speaks and leans back when the other does, sends observable signals.
On Zoom, those signals are either amplified or hidden. An adjudicator with camera off is invisible. One who is multitasking may not be noticed. Pre-hearing written remarks in case management communications can surface in ways that might not have been recorded before.
And the parties themselves are at home, in their own spaces, separated from each other and from the decision-maker. The shared physical experience of a public hearing — which has always been part of what makes adjudication legitimate, not just technically fair — is absent.
---
The labour board deference case
Also this week, the Divisional Court issued a decision reinforcing that courts should defer to the Ontario Labour Relations Board’s findings on workplace disputes. The case involved a construction industry work assignment dispute — technical, expert-driven, squarely within the OLRB’s institutional competence.
But it reminded me of the flip side of the bias question: tribunals that are expert in their field are given deference precisely because* they know things courts don’t. The moment a party starts alleging bias without real evidence, they risk asking a court to substitute its own judgment for that expert judgment — which is not what judicial review is for.
The reasonable apprehension of bias test is calibrated to distinguish genuine fairness concerns from tactical attempts to relitigate outcomes.
---
What I’m watching
As administrative hearings become increasingly digital and informal, the procedural fairness frameworks we have built around in-person adjudication are going to be tested. The bias test — built on the idea of the informed observer watching the process — assumes there is something to observe.
Zoom hearings are good for access. Whether they are equally good for perceived legitimacy is a question I don’t think the doctrine has fully answered yet.
---
See also: Baker v Canada, 1999 SCC 40, para 46 · Ontario Labour Relations Board · Tribunals Ontario — Remote Hearings

entries.body:
---
Why Did the Landlord Tenant Board Survive the s.96 Challenge?
TL;DR
The modern Landlord and Tenant Board (LTB) survived its constitutional challenge under s.96 of the Constitution Act, 1867 because (1) landlord-tenant jurisdiction was historically shared — not exclusively Superior Court — and (2) the LTB's adjudicative powers are genuinely incidental to a comprehensive housing policy scheme. An earlier version of the body (the 1981 Residential Tenancies Commission) was struck down for doing exactly what the LTB now avoids: wholesale replacement of court jurisdiction with no broader purpose.
Why s.96 matters for administrative tribunals
Section 96 of the Constitution Act, 1867 vests the appointment of Superior Court judges in the federal government. The Supreme Court has interpreted this as an implied constitutional guarantee: provincial legislatures cannot strip Superior Courts of their core judicial functions and hand them to administrative bodies.
But this creates a deep tension. Modern governance requires administrative tribunals to make quasi-judicial decisions,adjudicating disputes between landlords and tenants, employers and employees, immigration applicants and the Crown. If s.96 prohibited all such delegation, the administrative state could not function.
The courts' solution is a structured test that separates permissible delegation from unconstitutional court-stripping.
1. The constitutional framework: the three-step test
The governing framework emerged from [Reference re Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, [1981] 1 SCR 714](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1981/1981canscc20/1981canscc20.html) and was refined in subsequent cases. Courts ask three questions in sequence:

If Step 1 is "no" — the jurisdiction was never exclusively Superior Court — there is no s.96 problem at all. If Steps 1 and 2 are both "yes" but Step 3 is also "yes" (the adjudication is truly incidental to a broader scheme), the delegation is constitutional.
The critical insight: s.96 protects the core of Superior Court jurisdiction, not every task courts happened to perform in 1867.
2. Why the 1981 Commission failed
In the Ontario Residential Tenancies Act Reference, the Supreme Court struck down the Ontario Residential Tenancy Commission.
The Commission had been given virtually exclusive jurisdiction over landlord-tenant disputes — including the power to order eviction. The Court found:
Step 1  — Eviction orders were historically within the exclusive jurisdiction of Superior Courts at Confederation.
Step 2 — The Commission's adjudicative process was judicial in character: adversarial, rights-determining, binding.
Step 3 (fails the test) — The Commission's adjudicative functions were not incidental to a broader scheme. Adjudication was the scheme. It was, in Justice Dickson's words, "a wholesale removal of jurisdiction" from Superior Courts — exactly what s.96 prohibits.
The body existed to replace courts, not to carry out a wider regulatory purpose. That is the constitutional defect.
3. Why the modern LTB passes
The modern LTB, established under the Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (Ontario), was carefully structured to avoid repeating this failure.
A. Historically shared jurisdiction (Step 1 reassessed)
Later jurisprudence — particularly [Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1996/1996canscc15/1996canscc15.html) — confirmed that many landlord-tenant matters were never exclusively within Superior Court jurisdiction at Confederation. County courts, magistrates' courts, and other inferior bodies handled landlord-tenant disputes widely in 1867. Where the historical jurisdiction was shared, Step 1 yields "no" — and there is no s.96 problem for those matters.
B. Genuinely incidental adjudication (Step 3)
The LTB operates within a comprehensive legislative scheme with objectives far exceeding eviction adjudication:
Balancing the rights and obligations of landlords and tenants
Promoting access to safe and affordable housing
Providing mediation and alternative dispute resolution
Regulating rental housing conditions and standards
Establishing coherent landlord-tenant policy
The LTB's power to issue eviction orders is subsidiary and incidental to these broader goals — the precise situation Step 3 permits. Eviction authority serves the scheme; it does not constitute the scheme.
C. Judicial oversight preserved
The LTB is not a shadow court — it is subject to judicial review by the Divisional Court (Ontario Superior Court of Justice). The courts remain the ultimate guarantors of legality. This ongoing oversight relationship is what distinguishes an administrative body from an unconstitutional court-replacement.
4. The broader principle from MacMillan Bloedel
In [MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1995/1995canscc100/1995canscc100.html), the SCC drew a critical distinction: s.96 protects the core jurisdiction of Superior Courts — the inherent supervisory jurisdiction over lower courts and the fundamental rights of individuals — not every function Superior Courts happened to exercise in 1867.
Administrative bodies may validly adjudicate disputes, award remedies, and make binding orders — provided:
The jurisdiction was historically shared (not exclusively Superior Court), or
The judicial function is genuinely incidental to a broader scheme, and
The body remains subject to judicial oversight through JR.
The LTB satisfies all three. The 1981 Commission failed on the second and third.
Key takeaways
Section 96 creates an implied protection for the core of Superior Court jurisdiction — provinces cannot wholesale transfer it to administrative bodies.
The three-step test asks: is the jurisdiction historically exclusive, judicial in character, and not incidental to a broader scheme?
The 1981 Commission failed because adjudication was its primary and only purpose — a direct court-replacement with no regulatory surround.
The modern LTB passes because: much of its jurisdiction was historically shared; its adjudicative functions are genuinely incidental to a comprehensive housing policy scheme; and it remains subject to judicial review.
The trend is toward generosity: courts have recognized that a functional administrative state requires some overlap between ADM powers and traditional court functions — provided judicial oversight remains intact.
Sources and further reading
Key cases
[Reference re Ontario Residential Tenancies Act, [1981] 1 SCR 714](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1981/1981canscc20/1981canscc20.html) — original commission struck down
[Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1996/1996canscc15/1996canscc15.html) — updated framework; historical shared jurisdiction
[MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1995/1995canscc100/1995canscc100.html) — core jurisdiction concept
Legislation
Constitution Act, 1867, s.96
Residential Tenancies Act, 2006 (Ontario)
Further reading

Two adjudicators looking at the same AGCO licence revocation — high stakes for the individual, a statutory scheme that contemplates adjudication, significant personal and economic consequences — might still reach different conclusions about whether an oral hearing is required or whether written submissions suffice. For international students, this has direct relevance. Immigration appeals, refugee claims, and permit refusals under judicial review can take years to resolve. During that period, a person's status is precarious, their ability to plan is constrained, and they may be unable to access certain benefits or employment rights.

What "inordinate" means in the immigration context — where case complexity, IRCC resourcing, and the volume of applications compound the timeline — remains to be worked out in future cases. But the doctrinal recognition that process has a temporal dimension, not just a procedural one, is a real and recent shift that the seven-case evolution this week traced did not fully anticipate.

entries.body:
Week 8 Reflection: How Did We Get a Duty of Fairness, and What Does It Require?
Admin Law | Week of March 3, 2026
This week traced the historical evolution of procedural fairness in administrative law — from a narrow common law principle confined to "judicial" functions, through the SCC's mid-century expansion into a general duty of fairness, to the contextual synthesis in Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817 and the more recent recognition that delay itself can be a fairness violation. The key cases ran from Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) through Ridge v Baldwin [1964], Nicholson [1979], Martineau [1980], Cardinal [1985], and Knight v Indian Head School Division [1990], arriving at Baker's five-factor framework. The week closed with Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz (2022 SCC 29) on the question of delay. The central thread was how the doctrine moved from a category-based test — asking what type of function is being performed — to an interest-based, contextual approach asking what stakes are involved and what process is adequate to those stakes.
Abrametz is both course material and a live case worth understanding on its own terms. Peter Abrametz was a Saskatchewan lawyer found guilty of conduct unbecoming — making high-interest loans to vulnerable clients and issuing cheques to a fictitious person. Disciplinary proceedings commenced in 2012; the matter was not concluded until 2019. The Saskatchewan Court of Appeal stayed his disbarment on the basis that the delay amounted to an abuse of process. The SCC reversed 8-1, with Justice Rowe writing for the majority: the 71-month delay was long but not inordinate, given the complexity of the matter and Abrametz's own contribution to the timeline. Administrative law commentators noted that Abrametz lost on the facts but the case remains significant for articulating when delay can constitute abuse of process — a question courts had not previously addressed as squarely. As the HRLSC summarized, the threshold is high: significant prejudice caused by delay that is, on all the circumstances, inordinate.
Understanding why the doctrine reached this point requires tracking the evolution the course outlined. Cooper v Wandsworth (1863) established that even a statute silent on procedure did not eliminate the common law hearing right. "No man shall be condemned unheard" was treated as a foundational presumption. But for a long time after Cooper, courts confined that principle to "judicial" and "quasi-judicial" functions, refusing to apply it to decisions characterized as "administrative" or "ministerial." Whether you were entitled to any hearing at all depended on a formal classification that different courts reached differently for essentially identical bodies.
Ridge v Baldwin [1964] AC 40 began dismantling the category approach by asking instead whether the decision affected a person's rights or interests. The SCC's Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners [1979] 1 SCR 311 brought this to Canadian law, introducing a freestanding "duty of fairness" applicable without classifying the function as quasi-judicial. Martineau v Matsqui Institution [1980] 1 SCR 602 and Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution [1985] 2 SCR 643 made it a general common law rule applicable to any administrative decision affecting rights, interests, or privileges. Knight v Indian Head School Division [1990] 1 SCR 653 added the spectrum — the content of the duty varies from minimal process for broad policy choices to robust process for individual, rights-affecting decisions.
Baker synthesized all of this into five contextual factors. What's important for applying them is that they don't produce a formula. Two adjudicators looking at the same AGCO licence revocation — high stakes for the individual, a statutory scheme that contemplates adjudication, significant personal and economic consequences — might still reach different conclusions about whether an oral hearing is required or whether written submissions suffice. The factors structure the judgment without replacing it.
Abrametz then added the dimension the earlier cases hadn't squarely addressed: duration is itself a component of the fairness question, not just the procedural steps within a hearing. For international students, this has direct relevance. Immigration appeals, refugee claims, and permit refusals under judicial review can take years to resolve. During that period, a person's status is precarious, their ability to plan is constrained, and they may be unable to access certain benefits or employment rights. Abrametz confirms that if delay is inordinate and causes significant prejudice, it is not merely a policy failure but a legal wrong. What "inordinate" means in the immigration context — where case complexity, IRCC resourcing, and the volume of applications compound the timeline — remains to be worked out in future cases. But the doctrinal recognition that process has a temporal dimension, not just a procedural one, is a real and recent shift that the seven-case evolution this week traced did not fully anticipate.
Cases referenced: Law Society of Saskatchewan v Abrametz, 2022 SCC 29 | Cooper v Wandsworth Board of Works (1863) 143 ER 414 | Ridge v Baldwin, [1964] AC 40 | Nicholson v Haldimand-Norfolk Regional Police Commissioners, [1979] 1 SCR 311 | Martineau v Matsqui Institution, [1980] 1 SCR 602 | Cardinal v Director of Kent Institution, [1985] 2 SCR 643 | Knight v Indian Head School Division, [1990] 1 SCR 653 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
---

entries.title: Week 7: When an Agency’s Own Words Become a Legal Obligation — Legitimate Expectations

rich_text.text: Week 7 Reflection: Legitimate Expectations and the PGWP Policy Shift

Whether that translates into a legal entitlement depends on whether the materials they relied on were sufficiently unqualified. If that's right, the doctrine provides no meaningful protection against exactly the kind of policy shift that caused the most harm in 2024.

Week 7 Reflection: Legitimate Expectations and the PGWP Policy Shift
Admin Law | Week of February 24, 2026
This week the course covered legitimate expectations — the doctrine that recognizes, in certain circumstances, a legal entitlement to procedural protection when a public body has made a clear representation that a person reasonably relied on. The key cases were Mavi v Canada (2011 SCC 30), which tied legal weight to formal undertakings and confirmed the procedural-only scope of the doctrine in Canada; Agraira v Canada (2013 SCC 36), which extended the doctrine's reach to published ministerial guidelines where sufficiently clear and consistent; and Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817, which emphasized that the nature of the decision and the degree of individual reliance are relevant to how the duty operates. The threshold the doctrine requires — a representation that is clear, unambiguous, and unqualified — was a central point of discussion, and the week closed with an open question: what happens when government communications are carefully crafted to be informative but not unconditional?
On September 18, 2024, IRCC announced significant changes to Post-Graduation Work Permit eligibility, including a new requirement that PGWP applicants' programs align with specific fields linked to labour market needs — STEM, healthcare, agriculture, trades, and transportation. Programs in hospitality, social services, and the arts were conspicuously absent. As Green and Spiegel summarized, the transition protection was narrowly defined: students who had applied for their initial study permit before November 1, 2024 were exempt; those enrolled in a program but who hadn't yet submitted that application were not. Protests followed in Toronto, Brampton, Vancouver, and Montreal, with students carrying signs reading "We deserve a future." People had enrolled in specific programs because they understood those programs would lead to a PGWP. That understanding was shaped, in meaningful part, by what IRCC had publicly communicated.
Running this through the week's doctrine is instructive, and the analysis doesn't produce a confident answer. The doctrine's first requirement is a representation that is clear, unambiguous, and unqualified. IRCC's published PGWP materials have always come with caveats — that rules are subject to change, that programs must continue to meet requirements, that no guarantee of permanent rules is offered. Under the Mavi framework, which emphasized formal, specific undertakings made to identified individuals, IRCC's general web publications look weak as a basis for legitimate expectations. There was no signed undertaking, no individual commitment, nothing resembling the sponsorship agreements the SCC found sufficient in Mavi.
Agraira offers a slightly more promising angle. If IRCC's published guidance on PGWP eligibility was sufficiently clear and consistently applied over a long enough period, it might rise to the level of a practice or policy capable of generating expectations of process — at minimum, notice and an opportunity to be heard before the change took effect. But notice of what, exactly? The doctrine's procedural-only limit in Canada means that even a successful legitimate expectations claim would give affected students a process right, not a substantive entitlement to the old rules. And if IRCC went through that process — announced the change, took comments, then still adopted the new framework — the outcome for the students would be the same.
This is the part of the doctrine that I can't fully resolve from the course material. Baker's contextual approach asks about the stakes for the individual and the degree of reliance. For a student who enrolled in a hospitality program specifically because IRCC's materials indicated it would qualify for a PGWP — and who had no way to anticipate the September 2024 announcement — the reliance is real and the stakes are high. Whether that translates into a legal entitlement depends on whether the materials they relied on were sufficiently unqualified. The careful lawyering built into government publications — informative enough to be useful, qualified enough to preserve flexibility — may be designed precisely to sit below the legitimate expectations threshold. If that's right, the doctrine provides no meaningful protection against exactly the kind of policy shift that caused the most harm in 2024.
Cases referenced: Mavi v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 2011 SCC 30 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817
---

entries.body:
---
Is ServiceOntario a “Public Actor”?
A Practical Administrative Law Case Study
TL;DR
ServiceOntario performs public, statutory functions even when some locations are privately operated under contract. In principle, those decisions should remain subject to administrative law oversight—but the hybrid structure creates practical uncertainty about remedies and review pathways.
Why this question matters
Everyone in Ontario has used ServiceOntario for essential services (driver’s licence, health card, vehicle registration, etc.).
Because these services are foundational, many people assume ServiceOntario is purely “public.”
But administrative law asks a deeper question:
Is the actor legally public in function, authority, and accountability?
What triggered this inquiry
During a recent visit, I noticed a sign stating that the branch was operated by a private corporate agency under contract with the Ontario government.
That raises a core legal puzzle:
Can a privately operated location still be a public actor for administrative law purposes?
1) ServiceOntario’s legal structure (hybrid model)
ServiceOntario sits within the Ministry of Public and Business Service Delivery and Procurement.
Its delivery model is mixed:
Some locations are directly government-operated.
Many are operated by private businesses under contract.
Key point: these operators are not standard franchises in the legal sense.
The Arthur Wishart Act (Franchise Disclosure), 2000 excludes certain Crown service-contract arrangements from “franchise” treatment.
2) Administrative law framework
A. Nature of function test
ServiceOntario performs statutory, governmental functions:
issuing government identification,
registering vehicles,
administering health-card processes,
processing vital-statistics services.
These are public functions even if frontline delivery is contracted out.
B. Source of authority
Whether public employee or private operator, authority flows from statute.
The decision-maker is applying provincial legal criteria, not private business policy.
That strongly suggests administrative law principles still apply.
C. Public-private boundary
Outsourcing delivery does not automatically privatize accountability.
If a contractor is implementing a statutory scheme, courts may still treat those acts as public in substance.
Substance over form is the central principle here.
3) Practical challenge: How do you contest a bad decision?
Under the Judicial Review Procedure Act (Ontario), certain statutory decisions are reviewable.
But the hybrid model complicates litigation strategy:
Who is the proper respondent: Ministry, contractor, or both?
Which legal frame applies: public law, contract, or mixed?
Can mandamus-style remedies run effectively against private operators?
How should contractor action be attributed to government?
4) Alternative pathways beyond JR
Depending on facts, possible avenues include:
Internal administrative reconsideration
Ontario Ombudsman complaint
Human rights complaint (if discrimination is involved)
Civil action (e.g., negligence-related loss)
5) Structural gap
Ontario’s framework has not fully clarified this model.
Compared with tribunal systems under the SPPA, there is limited direct guidance on:
procedural fairness at initial service points,
responsibility split between contractor and Ministry,
review route and standards.
Key takeaways
Public function remains public even when delivery is contracted out.
Accountability should track power, not organizational form.
Residents need clear challenge pathways when legal rights are affected.
Hybrid governance is growing, and doctrine must keep pace.
Conclusion
ServiceOntario’s functions are public in nature and statutory in source.
The contract model should not, in principle, shield decisions from administrative oversight.
Yet in practice, the hybrid structure creates uncertainty for affected individuals.
ServiceOntario is therefore a useful lens for a broader administrative law challenge:
how to preserve accountability as governments modernize service delivery.
---
Sources and further reading
Legislation
Public Service of Ontario Act, 2006
Statutory Powers Procedure Act
Judicial Review Procedure Act
Arthur Wishart Act (Franchise Disclosure), 2000
Public sources
Government of Ontario: private ServiceOntario centre information
Public references documenting ServiceOntario’s 2006 launch and hybrid model
Concepts
Procedural fairness (Baker framework)
Standards of review
Public-function analysis for private entities
Scope and availability of judicial review

entries.title: Internal Guidelines: tension between jurisprudence towards soft law for ADMs
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Internal Guidelines: When ADM "Soft Law" Collides with the Rule of Law

Administrative decision-makers routinely develop internal guidelines, manuals, and policies — what scholars call "soft law" — to bring consistency to their discretionary decisions. These instruments are legally powerful but democratically unrecognized. They create three live tensions in administrative law: between consistency and fettering of discretion, between institutional coherence and individual justice, and between legal certainty and democratic accountability.

IRCC (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada) publishes hundreds of pages of internal operational guidelines on its website. Parole boards maintain internal policy directives. The AGCO issues compliance guidelines for licensees. The Canada Revenue Agency has interpretation bulletins.

entries.body:
Internal Guidelines: When ADM "Soft Law" Collides with the Rule of Law
TL;DR
Administrative decision-makers routinely develop internal guidelines, manuals, and policies — what scholars call "soft law" — to bring consistency to their discretionary decisions. These instruments are legally powerful but democratically unrecognized. They create three live tensions in administrative law: between consistency and fettering of discretion, between institutional coherence and individual justice, and between legal certainty and democratic accountability.
---
What triggered this observation
IRCC (Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada) publishes hundreds of pages of internal operational guidelines on its website. Parole boards maintain internal policy directives. The AGCO issues compliance guidelines for licensees. The Canada Revenue Agency has interpretation bulletins.
These documents look like law — they are detailed, authoritative, and routinely followed by decision-makers — but they are not statutes and they are not regulations. They were not enacted by the legislature. They are not subject to parliamentary scrutiny. They can be changed overnight.
And yet they govern tens of thousands of decisions every year, affecting immigration applicants, parolees, licensees, and taxpayers.
---
What is "soft law" in the administrative context?
Administrative "soft law" refers to rules, policies, guidelines, directives, and manuals that:
Are produced by an ADM or its enabling ministry
Structure how the ADM exercises its statutory discretion
Are not formally enacted as regulations under the Regulations Act or its federal equivalent
Do not have the force of law in the same sense as a statute or regulation
Common examples:
IRCC Operational Manuals (governing H&C applications, refugee proceedings, and more)
Parole Board of Canada Policy Manual
AGCO compliance guidelines for alcohol and cannabis licensees
CBSA enforcement bulletins
CRTC policy positions and frameworks
---
Tension 1: Consistency vs. Fettering of Discretion
The strongest argument for internal guidelines is that they promote consistency. Decision-makers facing materially identical facts should reach materially similar outcomes. Consistency is itself a value of the rule of law.
But consistency becomes unlawful when a decision-maker applies a guideline rigidly, without genuinely considering whether individual circumstances warrant a different result. This is the doctrine of fettering of discretion.
In [Maple Lodge Farms v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1982/1982canscc97/1982canscc97.html), the Supreme Court confirmed: an ADM that exercises statutory discretion must actually exercise it. An ADM that simply applies a policy as if it were mandatory has surrendered its statutory mandate to the policy's drafters — and that mandate belongs to the ADM alone, by virtue of the enabling statute.
The rule: guidelines may structure discretion; they cannot replace it. The decision-maker must always be open to departing from the guideline when the specific facts justify it.
Fettering is a ground of judicial review — a decision that mechanically applies a policy without genuine engagement with the individual case can be quashed.
---
Tension 2: Baker and the Legal Weight of Guidelines
In Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), 1999 SCC 40, the Supreme Court made a landmark ruling on the legal relevance of internal guidelines.
Baker challenged a deportation order made despite her long residence in Canada and her Canadian-born children. The immigration officer had guidelines that directed attention to "the best interests of the child" — guidelines the officer had plainly not applied. Justice L'Heureux-Dubé held:
Guidelines can give rise to legitimate expectations — if an ADM has committed to following a particular process or applying a particular standard, individuals can reasonably expect it will do so.
Guidelines inform procedural fairness obligations — what the duty of fairness requires in a given case is assessed partly against what the ADM's own guidelines say it will do.
Guidelines do not bind the substantive outcome, but they can be relevant to whether the outcome was reached in a fair and reasonable manner.
Baker thus elevated soft law to a legally significant role — not as binding rules, but as contextual markers of what fairness and reasonable discretion require in practice.
---
Tension 3: Agraira and the Democratic Deficit
In Agraira v Canada (Minister of Public Safety), 2013 SCC 36, the SCC reviewed a ministerial guideline on "national interest" exemptions for inadmissible permanent residence applicants. The Court held that the Minister's decision had to be assessed against the guideline he had published — the guideline was part of the legitimate framework for reviewing how discretion was exercised.
This is legally significant, but it deepens a structural problem: ministerial guidelines effectively function as law, made without legislative process.
Regulations must go through:
Drafting and internal review
Cabinet approval
Tabling in Parliament (federal) or filing with the Registrar (Ontario)
Publication in the Canada Gazette or Ontario Gazette
Parliamentary scrutiny
Internal guidelines go through none of this. A Minister can issue, revise, or withdraw a guideline without public consultation, without parliamentary debate, and without judicial review of the guideline itself (only of the decision made under it).
This is what the course calls the "design" tension in administrative law: the efficiency and expertise of the administrative state come at a cost to transparency and democratic legitimacy. Soft law is where that trade-off is sharpest.
---
Vavilov and the Soft Law Problem
In Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65, the SCC emphasized that a reasonable decision must be justified with reference to the legal and factual constraints binding on the decision-maker. Internal guidelines are one such constraint.
Where an ADM departs from its own published guidelines without explanation, that departure may itself be a basis for finding the decision unreasonable — not because the guideline is legally binding, but because unexplained inconsistency with stated policy undermines the transparency and coherence that Vavilov demands. The decision-maker who ignores their own framework cannot claim to be reasoning in good faith.
The flip side: an ADM who rigidly follows a guideline without considering the individual facts has fettered their discretion. Vavilov demands genuine reasoning either way.
---
A live access-to-justice problem
The soft law problem is not merely academic. It has real consequences for real people:
A refugee claimant who does not know IRCC's operational guidelines cannot effectively challenge a decision that misapplied them — the guidelines are often the only window into the decision-making framework.
A cannabis licensee who does not know AGCO's unpublished enforcement priorities cannot know what conduct puts their licence at risk.
A person subject to a parole directive has no legislative avenue through which to challenge its content — only the decision made under it.
Administrative law partially addresses this through procedural fairness, legitimate expectations, and the Vavilov reasonableness standard. But the democratic deficit created by soft law remains one of the deepest structural tensions in the field.
---
Key takeaways
Soft law (guidelines, policies, manuals) is not legally binding in the same sense as statutes or regulations — but it structurally governs millions of decisions.
Fettering doctrine: an ADM must always exercise genuine discretion. Guidelines may structure but cannot replace it (Maple Lodge Farms).
Baker: internal guidelines can give rise to legitimate expectations and shape what procedural fairness requires in a given case.
Agraira: ministerial guidelines form part of the legitimate framework for assessing whether discretion was reasonably exercised.
Vavilov: unexplained departure from published guidelines may render a decision unreasonable for lack of transparency and coherence.
The democratic deficit remains: soft law governs at scale without the legislative scrutiny that binds formal regulations.
---

Key cases

[Maple Lodge Farms v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2](https://www.canlii.org/en/ca/scc/doc/1982/1982canscc97/1982canscc97.html)
Agraira v Canada (Minister of Public Safety), 2013 SCC 36
Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
Kanthasamy v Canada (Citizenship and Immigration), 2015 SCC 61

Regulations Act (Ontario)

entries.title: Week 5: Building to Survive — How the LTB Was Designed Around a Constitutional Challenge
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Week 5 Reflection: Did the LTB Actually Survive s. 96 — or Just Pass a Low Bar?

This week the course addressed the constitutional constraints on what provincial legislatures can delegate to administrative tribunals — specifically, the s. 96 challenge to the Landlord and Tenant Board. The framework is a three-step test from the

(Ontario) [1981] 1 SCR 714: characterize the power being delegated; ask whether it broadly conforms to a jurisdiction "exclusively or primarily" held by superior courts at Confederation; and if so, determine whether it is embedded in a broader administrative scheme that warrants specialization. The Ontario scheme failed in 1981 — the tribunal's eviction and possession powers resembled superior court jurisdiction too closely, and the administrative context wasn't developed enough to save it. The legislature redesigned. The modern LTB, under the
, survived s. 96 scrutiny because the new scheme was sufficiently distinct from common law landlord-tenant adjudication to clear the third step, a result confirmed by the
(Nova Scotia) [1996] 1 SCR 186. The week's question was what that survival actually guarantees.

, the result of a three-year investigation into the LTB. The findings documented a backlog of more than 38,000 cases, median wait times of seven to eight months for landlord applications and up to two years for some tenant matters.
CBC News described the LTB as "fundamentally failing"
argued that the digital-first hearing model deepened rather than resolved the crisis.
published ongoing numbers. Reading this alongside the week's cases, the question that kept surfacing was: the three-step test told us the LTB is constitutionally
— but what does that validity actually say about the system described in the Ombudsman's report?
The answer the course framework gives is precise and limited. The three-step test asks whether the

of power can be delegated. It says nothing about how well the power must be exercised or what resources must be allocated to exercise it adequately. The LTB's 2023 backlog was not a constitutional failure. Every step of the 1981 test was satisfied. What failed was the gap between what the statute promised — accessible, timely dispute resolution — and what the institution delivered in practice. These are different questions, and the constitutional framework only speaks to the first.
[1995] 4 SCR 725 add the structural limits that bracket what the legislature can do once it delegates.
protects judicial review of jurisdictional error;

prevents the full abolition of superior court oversight. Together they ensure that even a constitutionally valid tribunal remains judicially accountable for excess of authority and that courts retain an irreducible supervisory role. But neither case speaks to how fast the tribunal must work or how many adjudicators it must employ. The constitutional safety valves are about unlawful action, not slow action.
This is where the analysis from class meets a dimension the course doesn't fully address. The Ombudsman's report documented not just delay but the

of delay: tenants unable to get orders enforcing maintenance obligations, people in unsafe housing waiting months for hearings that the statute theoretically made available to them. The constitutional survival of the LTB tells those tenants the

forum exists. It tells them nothing about whether they will receive justice in any practical timeframe. For international students in Ontario — who disproportionately rent, often in housing with maintenance issues, and who may be less familiar with how to assert LTB rights — the question of functional adequacy is not abstract.

entries.body:
Week 5 Reflection: Did the LTB Actually Survive s. 96 — or Just Pass a Low Bar?
Admin Law | Week of February 10, 2026
This week the course addressed the constitutional constraints on what provincial legislatures can delegate to administrative tribunals — specifically, the s. 96 challenge to the Landlord and Tenant Board. The framework is a three-step test from the Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979 (Ontario) [1981] 1 SCR 714: characterize the power being delegated; ask whether it broadly conforms to a jurisdiction "exclusively or primarily" held by superior courts at Confederation; and if so, determine whether it is embedded in a broader administrative scheme that warrants specialization. The Ontario scheme failed in 1981 — the tribunal's eviction and possession powers resembled superior court jurisdiction too closely, and the administrative context wasn't developed enough to save it. The legislature redesigned. The modern LTB, under the Residential Tenancies Act, 2006, survived s. 96 scrutiny because the new scheme was sufficiently distinct from common law landlord-tenant adjudication to clear the third step, a result confirmed by the Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia) [1996] 1 SCR 186. The week's question was what that survival actually guarantees.
In May 2023, Ontario's Ombudsman released Administrative Justice Delayed, Fairness Denied, the result of a three-year investigation into the LTB. The findings documented a backlog of more than 38,000 cases, median wait times of seven to eight months for landlord applications and up to two years for some tenant matters. CBC News described the LTB as "fundamentally failing". The Advocacy Centre for Tenants Ontario argued that the digital-first hearing model deepened rather than resolved the crisis. Tribunal Watch Ontario published ongoing numbers. Reading this alongside the week's cases, the question that kept surfacing was: the three-step test told us the LTB is constitutionally valid — but what does that validity actually say about the system described in the Ombudsman's report?
The answer the course framework gives is precise and limited. The three-step test asks whether the type of power can be delegated. It says nothing about how well the power must be exercised or what resources must be allocated to exercise it adequately. The LTB's 2023 backlog was not a constitutional failure. Every step of the 1981 test was satisfied. What failed was the gap between what the statute promised — accessible, timely dispute resolution — and what the institution delivered in practice. These are different questions, and the constitutional framework only speaks to the first.
Crevier v AG Quebec [1981] 2 SCR 220 and MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson [1995] 4 SCR 725 add the structural limits that bracket what the legislature can do once it delegates. Crevier protects judicial review of jurisdictional error; MacMillan Bloedel prevents the full abolition of superior court oversight. Together they ensure that even a constitutionally valid tribunal remains judicially accountable for excess of authority and that courts retain an irreducible supervisory role. But neither case speaks to how fast the tribunal must work or how many adjudicators it must employ. The constitutional safety valves are about unlawful action, not slow action.
This is where the analysis from class meets a dimension the course doesn't fully address. The Ombudsman's report documented not just delay but the effects of delay: tenants unable to get orders enforcing maintenance obligations, people in unsafe housing waiting months for hearings that the statute theoretically made available to them. The constitutional survival of the LTB tells those tenants the right forum exists. It tells them nothing about whether they will receive justice in any practical timeframe. For international students in Ontario — who disproportionately rent, often in housing with maintenance issues, and who may be less familiar with how to assert LTB rights — the question of functional adequacy is not abstract.
Cases referenced: Reference re Residential Tenancies Act, 1979 (Ontario), [1981] 1 SCR 714 | Reference re Residential Tenancies Act (Nova Scotia), [1996] 1 SCR 186 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65
---

This week the course introduced soft law — policies, guidelines, and internal instructions that agencies use to structure discretion without formal rule-making — and the fettering doctrine that governs when those tools cross a legal line. The key framework came from

The doctrinal tension is real: ignoring guidelines can be arbitrary; following them too rigidly is fettering. The week left the question of where exactly that line sits as one the cases don't fully resolve.

That tension maps almost perfectly onto something immigration lawyers have been raising publicly for years. But here is the difficulty the cases expose — from the outside, a thoughtful application of the framework and a mechanical one can produce identical output. Both result in the same refusal letter citing the same criteria. The

The reasonableness standard requires decision-makers to produce reasons that actually grapple with the key considerations in the record — including why the guideline criteria were or weren't met for this particular applicant. A refusal that recites the PDI language without engaging with the specific evidence is more vulnerable to a finding of unreasonableness under

But the framework still doesn't address an earlier problem. The PDIs themselves are not easily accessible to applicants. If you don't know what criteria the officer is applying, you cannot structure your application to engage with those criteria, and you cannot identify on review whether the officer applied them thoughtfully or mechanically. The fettering doctrine addresses the quality of the individual decision. The opacity of the underlying framework is a problem at a different stage — one that produces the conditions for fettering without being fettering itself. Whether that opacity is an administrative law problem or just a transparency policy problem, I haven't found the cases directly addressing.
