Admin Law | Week of February 3, 2026
This week the course introduced soft law — policies, guidelines, and internal instructions that agencies use to structure discretion without formal rule-making — and the fettering doctrine that governs when those tools cross a legal line. The key framework came from Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada [1982] 2 SCR 2: when a decision-maker applies a policy so mechanically that it substitutes the policy for actual judgment, the discretion Parliament delegated is not exercised at all. But the week also covered Baker v Canada [1999] 2 SCR 817, which treated ministerial guidelines as legitimate and important context for structuring discretion — and Agraira v Canada (2013 SCC 36), which suggested that a reasonable officer would engage seriously with published guidelines. The doctrinal tension is real: ignoring guidelines can be arbitrary; following them too rigidly is fettering. The week left the question of where exactly that line sits as one the cases don't fully resolve.
That tension maps almost perfectly onto something immigration lawyers have been raising publicly for years. In 2022 and 2023, concerns were documented about how IRCC officers were assessing the "genuineness of studies" requirement for study permit applications. The operative criteria were drawn from internal Program Delivery Instructions — internal documents not published as regulations. The International Bar Association has noted that "genuineness" refusals are among the most common and hardest to challenge, often arriving as boilerplate with no specific explanation. Lawyers have used access-to-information requests and procedural fairness letter responses to piece together what criteria were actually applied, sometimes finding that officers appeared to be ticking boxes — travel history, financial ties, family circumstances — without engaging with the full picture.
This is the Maple Lodge Farms problem in a contemporary setting. The PDI provides a structured assessment framework; the officer applies it to a file; a refusal is issued. The question the course framework asks is: was the framework used as a tool for judgment, or as a substitute for judgment? A refusal that says "the applicant has not demonstrated sufficient ties to their home country" without engaging with the specific evidence in the file looks more like the latter. But here is the difficulty the cases expose — from the outside, a thoughtful application of the framework and a mechanical one can produce identical output. Both result in the same refusal letter citing the same criteria. The Maple Lodge fettering concern is partly an evidentiary problem that reviewing courts have to reconstruct after the fact.
Vavilov (2019 SCC 65) gives one answer to this. The reasonableness standard requires decision-makers to produce reasons that actually grapple with the key considerations in the record — including why the guideline criteria were or weren't met for this particular applicant. A refusal that recites the PDI language without engaging with the specific evidence is more vulnerable to a finding of unreasonableness under Vavilov than one showing genuine individual consideration. In that sense, Vavilov operationalizes the Maple Lodge/Baker tension: it creates an accountability mechanism through the reasons requirement that should reveal, at least partially, whether the officer was exercising genuine judgment or applying a checklist.
But the framework still doesn't address an earlier problem. The PDIs themselves are not easily accessible to applicants. If you don't know what criteria the officer is applying, you cannot structure your application to engage with those criteria, and you cannot identify on review whether the officer applied them thoughtfully or mechanically. The fettering doctrine addresses the quality of the individual decision. The opacity of the underlying framework is a problem at a different stage — one that produces the conditions for fettering without being fettering itself. Whether that opacity is an administrative law problem or just a transparency policy problem, I haven't found the cases directly addressing.
Cases referenced: Maple Lodge Farms Ltd v Canada, [1982] 2 SCR 2 | Baker v Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration), [1999] 2 SCR 817 | Agraira v Canada (Public Safety and Emergency Preparedness), 2013 SCC 36 | Canada (Minister of Citizenship and Immigration) v Vavilov, 2019 SCC 65

entries.body:
Admin Law | Week of January 27, 2026
This week the course turned to the design of administrative bodies, not just whether a body is public (we covered that) but how the internal architecture of a tribunal shapes the lawfulness and quality of its decisions. The key cases were Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v BC (2001 SCC 52), which held that structural features like member tenure are not constitutionally mandated but are legislative choices; Crevier v AG Quebec [1981] 2 SCR 220, which set the constitutional floor by protecting judicial review of jurisdictional errors from statutory ouster; and MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson [1995] 4 SCR 725, which established the outer hard limit — there is an irreducible core of superior court jurisdiction that provincial legislatures cannot abolish entirely. Together, these three cases define a spectrum: wide design latitude, a jurisdictional floor, and an outer limit. The week's central question was what that spectrum actually constrains in practice.
In December 2021, the AGCO suspended and moved to revoke the liquor licence of Zucchinis Cucina, an Oakville restaurant that had admitted unvaccinated dine-in patrons in violation of Ontario's proof-of-vaccination requirements. It was one of many enforcement actions that year, the AGCO's own account of its COVID-19 response describes Compliance Officials conducting multi-ministry enforcement sweeps of high-risk businesses. Some affected operators complained that actions came quickly with limited opportunity to respond, and that the AGCO's published progressive enforcement framework was not being consistently applied. The instinctive reaction is that the design should have built in more procedural protection. But applying the week's frameworks, what does that complaint actually translate to legally?
Start with Ocean Port. The AGCO's governing structure, a Commission setting policy, a Registrar applying it, a Licence Appeal Tribunal handling challenges, is entirely a product of the Alcohol and Gaming Commission of Ontario Act, 2019. Under Ocean Port, the legislature had full latitude to design that structure. Member tenure, the degree of Commission oversight over the Registrar, the separation (or lack thereof) between policy-setting and individual adjudication, these are all statutory choices, not constitutional requirements. If the design produces outcomes an operator thinks are unfair, Ocean Port says that's a legislative policy problem, not a constitutional one.
Crevier supplies the floor. If the AGCO Registrar acted beyond its statutory authority, for example, by invoking violations of public health orders enacted under different legislation as grounds for licence action, when the AGCO Act only authorizes action for violations of its own statutory conditions, then that jurisdictional excess is reviewable by superior courts regardless of any statutory language purporting to limit appeal. The floor is not about whether the design is good; it's about whether the tribunal stayed within its lawful authority. Whether the COVID enforcement actions crossed that line would require a careful reading of the Registrar's enabling provisions, and the AGCO doesn't appear to have faced a successful jurisdictional challenge on this basis.
MacMillan Bloedel adds the outer limit but doesn't change much for this scenario, the LAT provides an appeal route and courts retain review jurisdiction, so the scheme is nowhere near abolishing superior court oversight entirely.
What this analysis reveals is the gap the week's material raised but didn't fully close. Running the three cases against the Zucchinis Cucina scenario, the legal answer is probably that the AGCO's enforcement was constitutionally valid, within its statutory authority, and not an Ocean Port violation. But Law Society of Ontario v AA (2026 ONCA 47), decided just recently, suggests a separate inquiry runs alongside: even if a tribunal's design is constitutionally valid, its process in a specific case must be adequate to the stakes involved. The ONCA, reviewing a Law Society Tribunal discipline proceeding, asked whether the process fit what was at stake for the person subject to it. That's a Baker-style procedural fairness question, not a structural design question, and it operates whether or not the constitutional tests are satisfied. The harder problem for affected AGCO licensees may not be Ocean Port or Crevier but the content of procedural fairness, which is where next week's material picks up.
Cases referenced: Ocean Port Hotel Ltd v British Columbia, 2001 SCC 52 | Crevier v AG Quebec, [1981] 2 SCR 220 | MacMillan Bloedel Ltd v Simpson, [1995] 4 SCR 725 | Law Society of Ontario v AA, 2026 ONCA 47

entries.body:
Week 2 Reflection: What Makes a Decision "Governmental Enough" to Review?
Admin Law | Week of January 20, 2026
This week we moved to a question that follows directly from the source-of-power analysis: given that judicial review is meant for public law decision-makers, how do courts decide whether a particular body qualifies? The Wall test (2018 SCC 26) is the current answer, asking whether the decision-maker exercises a public power, meaning one that derives from legislation in a way that makes the function governmental in character. The course also traced the three pre-Wall lines that produced inconsistent results: the Lindenburger source-of-power line, the Air Canada v Toronto Port Authority multi-factor governmental-function line, and the McCaw/Lakeside Colony statutory-versus-private power line. Each captured something real; the problem was they sometimes pointed in different directions for the same body. Wall tried to bring coherence. The question I left class with was whether it succeeded, or just substituted one set of difficulties for another.
During the COVID-19 pandemic, a number of Ontario condominium corporations issued rules restricting common area access, guest policies, and elevator use for residents believed to be infectious. Some boards enforced these rules through significant fines. CBC News reported on a downtown Toronto condo that required proof of vaccination to use amenities, a move lawyers warned could be challenged. The Globe and Mail noted that condo boards were largely left to navigate on their own, without provincial guidance on what they could lawfully impose. A few residents wanted to challenge these decisions and had to figure out which forum applied. That question maps directly onto what we studied: was the condo board exercising a public power subject to judicial review, or a private power that only private law could address?
Apply the Wall framework: where does a condo board's authority come from? The Condominium Act creates the corporation and grants governance powers, but the statute is essentially enabling legislation for a community of private property owners to manage shared spaces. The relationship between a condo corporation and its unit owners looks far more like a contractual private arrangement than a government regulating the public. Under Wall, that probably takes it outside judicial review. Courts have upheld COVID condo policies as valid exercises of board authority — but through private law mechanisms (the Condominium Act's own dispute resolution framework and the Condominium Authority Tribunal), not judicial review.
Now run the pre-Wall lines against the same facts and see what happens. The Lindenburger source-of-power line would ask whether the authority derives from a statute — and the Condominium Act does provide the statutory basis, which might point toward reviewability. The Air Canada TPA multi-factor line would look at government funding (very little), government control (minimal), and public character of the function (debatable) — and would likely land on "not sufficiently governmental." The McCaw/Lakeside Colony line would ask whether the body exercises a statutory power specifically — and might reach a different conclusion than Wall if the board's enforcement authority is traced to the Condominium Act directly. The point is that the answer isn't stable across the frameworks, which is exactly the problem Wall was meant to fix.
Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral (2021 SCC 22) applied Wall to find that a religious community's expulsion of members was not reviewable — clean answer, significant consequences for the people expelled. As an international student, the institution with the most practical power over my daily life is the university, not a government agency. Universities are created by provincial statute, receive public funding, and make decisions about academic standing and discipline that directly affect study permit status. Wall and Aga have arguably made the private/public characterization harder to apply for these ambiguous middle-ground bodies. MacKinnon v University of Guelph (2025 FC 422) shows federal courts are still actively working through where universities sit. The Wall test gave us one question to ask instead of three, but the one question is not obviously easier to answer for bodies like universities that straddle the line.
Cases referenced: Wall v Independent Planning Association, 2018 SCC 26 | Aga v Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo Church of Canada St. Mary Cathedral, 2021 SCC 22 | MacKinnon v University of Guelph, 2025 FC 422

entries.title: Week 1: The ServiceOntario Agent — Who Is Actually Making the Decision?
author: Fukun

rich_text.text: Week 1 Reflection: Who Is Actually Making the Decision?

This week we started from the most basic question administrative law has to answer: what is an "administrative decision-maker," and how do we know when a body is one? The core answer the course introduced is the source-of-power test — a body exercises public law power when its authority derives from statute, and it is that statutory delegation that brings it within the reach of judicial review.

[1959] SCR 121 gave us the foundational illustration: a government official who used statutory power for improper purposes was held accountable precisely because the power was public. The authority was legislative; the accountability followed.
When I applied for my study permit last year, the first step was not submitting anything to IRCC, it was booking an appointment through

, a private company contracted by the government to collect biometrics and application documents. You pay a separate service fee to VFS, submit your materials at a commercial office, and then wait for IRCC to make the actual decision. What I didn't think about at the time was the accountability question embedded in this arrangement. IRCC's own

makes the legal position explicit: "VFS Global is not an agent of the Government of Canada but is a completely independent entity operating under contract." In other words, if documents are lost or biometrics incorrectly recorded, VFS's relationship with me is governed by private contract, not public law.

This is exactly where the source-of-power test runs into a gap that this week's material doesn't fully resolve. Run the test on VFS: where does its authority come from? The answer is a contract with IRCC, not the

. VFS doesn't assess applications or make eligibility decisions — it receives documents and collects biometrics. On the strict source-of-power analysis from class, that probably places VFS outside the definition of an administrative decision-maker entirely. It exercises no power delegated by public law; there is nothing to judicially review.

But think about what that means practically. VFS is a mandatory gateway to a statutory right. You cannot submit a study permit application to IRCC without going through VFS for biometrics. An error at that stage can delay or derail an application with consequences for status and future immigration history. The actual decision-maker (IRCC) had no role in causing the harm. And yet the person harmed — the applicant — has no direct public law remedy. Contract law gives IRCC a potential claim against VFS for service failures, but it does not give the applicant standing to claim anything.

The same structural problem appears with ServiceOntario. Ontario has contracted out vehicle and driver licensing services to private registry agents — pharmacies, car dealerships, private offices, for years. The

entries.body:
Week 1 Reflection: Who Is Actually Making the Decision?
Admin Law | Week of January 13, 2026
This week we started from the most basic question administrative law has to answer: what is an "administrative decision-maker," and how do we know when a body is one? The core answer the course introduced is the source-of-power test — a body exercises public law power when its authority derives from statute, and it is that statutory delegation that brings it within the reach of judicial review. Roncarelli v Duplessis [1959] SCR 121 gave us the foundational illustration: a government official who used statutory power for improper purposes was held accountable precisely because the power was public. The authority was legislative; the accountability followed.
When I applied for my study permit last year, the first step was not submitting anything to IRCC, it was booking an appointment through VFS Global, a private company contracted by the government to collect biometrics and application documents. You pay a separate service fee to VFS, submit your materials at a commercial office, and then wait for IRCC to make the actual decision. What I didn't think about at the time was the accountability question embedded in this arrangement. IRCC's own VFS consent form makes the legal position explicit: "VFS Global is not an agent of the Government of Canada but is a completely independent entity operating under contract." In other words, if documents are lost or biometrics incorrectly recorded, VFS's relationship with me is governed by private contract, not public law.
This is exactly where the source-of-power test runs into a gap that this week's material doesn't fully resolve. Run the test on VFS: where does its authority come from? The answer is a contract with IRCC, not the Immigration and Refugee Protection Act. VFS doesn't assess applications or make eligibility decisions — it receives documents and collects biometrics. On the strict source-of-power analysis from class, that probably places VFS outside the definition of an administrative decision-maker entirely. It exercises no power delegated by public law; there is nothing to judicially review.
But think about what that means practically. VFS is a mandatory gateway to a statutory right. You cannot submit a study permit application to IRCC without going through VFS for biometrics. An error at that stage can delay or derail an application with consequences for status and future immigration history. The actual decision-maker (IRCC) had no role in causing the harm. And yet the person harmed — the applicant — has no direct public law remedy. Contract law gives IRCC a potential claim against VFS for service failures, but it does not give the applicant standing to claim anything.
The same structural problem appears with ServiceOntario. Ontario has contracted out vehicle and driver licensing services to private registry agents — pharmacies, car dealerships, private offices, for years. The Auditor General of Ontario raised concerns about this model: when private operators make errors affecting people's licences, the path to correction is murky because the agents are not statutory decision-makers. The errors have real consequences for real people. But the source-of-power test as we studied it this week points to "no public law accountability" for the private operator, only for the ministry that designed the system.
This is what I keep coming back to: the source-of-power test tells us when administrative law applies to a decision-maker, but it is silent on the question of who is accountable for building a system that routes statutory rights through a private contractor. Roncarelli established that public authority creates public accountability,  but the corollary the case didn't need to address is what happens when public authority is exercised through private architecture. Whether that gap reflects a principled distinction between public decision-making and contracted service delivery, or just a feature of doctrine developed before widespread outsourcing, is something I haven't found an answer to yet.
Cases referenced: Roncarelli v Duplessis, [1959] SCR 121
